refashioned: The Master said, 'When rulers love propriety, the people respond readily to calls for service.'
elegant: When rulers honor propriety, the people serve willingly. Compliance follows respect.
distilled: People serve willingly when they see their rulers taking obligations seriously.
notes: Shang hao li (上好禮) — 'when those above love Li.' Voluntary compliance vs. forced obedience. The mechanism: when people see their leaders observing proper conduct, they trust the system enough to participate. Connects to FUALL's ideational domain of power: legitimacy produces voluntary cooperation.

refashioned: Zilu asked what makes a superior person. The Master said, 'Cultivating himself with reverential care.' 'Is that all?' 'Cultivating himself so as to bring peace to others.' 'Is that all?' 'Cultivating himself so as to bring peace to all the people. Even Yao and Shun found this difficult.'
elegant: First, cultivate yourself with reverence. Then, bring peace to those around you. Then, to all people. Even the sage-kings found that last step hard.
distilled: Self-cultivation expands outward — from personal discipline to peace for others to peace for all — and even the greatest rulers struggled with the final circle.
notes: Xiu ji yi jing (修己以敬) → xiu ji yi an ren (修己以安人) → xiu ji yi an bai xing (修己以安百姓). Three concentric circles of responsibility: self → intimates → all people. Yao (堯) and Shun (舜) — the sage-kings who represent the highest ideal of governance. Even they found universal peace difficult. Connects to FUALL's framework: wisdom begins with self-cultivation but aims at civilizational benefit.

refashioned: Yuan Rang squatted casually, waiting for the Master. Confucius said, 'In youth you were not respectful. In manhood you achieved nothing worth passing on. Now in old age you go on living — you are a pest.' And he rapped him on the shins with his staff.
elegant: Disrespectful in youth, useless in manhood, and still alive in old age. Confucius called him a pest and hit him with a stick.
distilled: A life that produces nothing at any stage and persists regardless is a burden, not an achievement.
notes: Yuan Rang (原壤) — traditionally identified as an old acquaintance of Confucius, not a disciple. Squatting (ji ju, 踞) was considered extremely disrespectful. Confucius's physical rebuke is unique in the Analects. The three failures (youth without humility, adulthood without contribution, old age without legacy) represent a complete waste of human potential.

refashioned: A boy from the village of Que served as Confucius's messenger. Someone asked, 'Has he made good progress?' The Master said, 'I notice he likes to sit in the seats of adults and walk alongside his elders rather than behind them. He is not someone seeking to learn — he just wants to grow up fast.'
elegant: The boy sat in adults' seats and walked beside his elders. Confucius said: 'He does not want to learn. He wants to skip ahead.'
distilled: The desire to appear mature is the opposite of the patience required to actually mature.
notes: The boy mimics the forms of adulthood without doing the work of development. This is a critique of premature ambition — confusing the appearance of advancement with actual growth. Connects to FUALL's Academy: genuine learning requires accepting one's current stage rather than performing a more advanced one.

refashioned: Duke Ling of Wei asked Confucius about military tactics. Confucius replied, 'I know about sacrificial vessels, but I have never studied warfare.' He left the next day. In the state of Chen, their provisions ran out, and his followers became too weak to stand. Zilu said bitterly, 'Must the superior person endure this too?' The Master said, 'The superior person endures hardship with composure. The inferior person, in hardship, loses all restraint.'
elegant: Asked about war, Confucius said he knew only ritual — and left. When famine came, his student asked: 'Must the superior person suffer like this?' 'Yes. But the inferior person, suffering, loses himself.'
distilled: The difference between the superior and inferior person is not whether they suffer but whether suffering breaks their character.
notes: Duke Ling of Wei (衛靈公) — see 14.20. Confucius refuses to advise on military matters, consistent with his emphasis on De (virtue) over force. The subsequent famine in Chen tests his own teaching: Junzi gu qiong (君子固窮) — 'the superior person is steadfast in hardship.' Xiaoren qiong si lan (小人窮斯濫) — 'the petty person in hardship runs wild.' Connects to FUALL's Wisdom: principles must hold under pressure or they are not principles.

refashioned: The Master said, 'Zigong, do you think I merely learn many things and remember them?' Zigong replied, 'Yes — is that not so?' The Master said, 'No. I seek one principle that threads through everything.'
elegant: 'You think I am a man of many facts? No. I seek the one thread that connects them all.'
distilled: The goal of learning is not accumulation but integration — finding the single principle that unifies everything.
notes: Yi yi guan zhi (一以貫之) — 'one thread running through all.' This is Confucius's most explicit statement about the unity of his teaching. The 'one thread' is traditionally interpreted as Zhong Shu (忠恕, loyalty and reciprocity) or simply Ren (仁). Connects directly to FUALL's framework: the project seeks a unified understanding (Wisdom = Reality + Reason + Right) rather than a collection of separate insights.

refashioned: The Master said, 'Yu, those who truly know virtue are few.'
elegant: Those who truly know virtue are few. The Master names no exceptions.
distilled: Genuine understanding of virtue is rare — even among those who pursue it.
notes: Zhi de zhe xian (知德者鮮) — 'those who know virtue are rare.' Addressed to Zilu (Yu). A sobering assessment from a teacher surrounded by students: even among the dedicated, deep understanding is uncommon.

refashioned: The Master said, 'Shun may be the best example of governing without effort. What did he do? He simply sat on his throne with gravity and reverence, facing south — and everything was in order.'
elegant: Shun governed without exertion. He sat with gravity, faced south, and the world ordered itself around him.
distilled: The highest governance is so well-founded that the ruler's mere presence is sufficient.
notes: Wu wei er zhi (無為而治) — 'governing through non-action.' Shun (舜) — the sage-king who exemplifies effortless governance. Nan mian (南面, facing south) — the ritual posture of the Chinese emperor. The concept parallels Daoist wu wei but with a Confucian emphasis: Shun's 'effortlessness' was the product of appointing the right people and maintaining proper institutions. Connects to FUALL's framework: the best-designed institutions require minimal intervention.

refashioned: Zizhang asked how to be valued wherever one goes. The Master said, 'Let your words be sincere and truthful, your actions honorable and careful — and this conduct will work even among uncivilized peoples. If your words lack sincerity and your actions lack honor, will you even be valued in your own neighborhood? When standing, imagine these principles right before you. When riding in a carriage, see them attached to the yoke. Then you can practice them anywhere.' Zizhang wrote these words on his sash.
elegant: Sincerity in speech, honor in action — these work everywhere, even among barbarians. Zizhang wrote it on his sash so he would never forget.
distilled: Universal respect is earned by two things: meaning what you say and doing what is right.
notes: Zhong xin (忠信, faithfulness and sincerity) and Du jing (篤敬, honorable carefulness) — Confucius's recipe for universal applicability. The visual advice (see them before you, attached to the yoke) is a practical mnemonic technique. Zizhang writing on his sash (shen, 紳) is one of the Analects' most vivid images of a student internalizing a teaching. Connects to FUALL's Right: genuine ethics are cross-cultural.

refashioned: The Master said, 'The historian Yu was truly straight — like an arrow whether the government was good or bad. And Qu Boyu is a truly superior person — in office when government is good, able to roll up his principles and keep them safe when it is bad.'
elegant: The historian was an arrow in all weather. Qu Boyu served when it was right and withdrew when it wasn't — rolling up his principles like a scroll to preserve them.
distilled: One form of integrity is unwavering directness; another is knowing when to serve and when to withdraw.
notes: Shi Yu (史魚) — a historiographer of Wei known for uncompromising integrity. Qu Boyu (蘧伯玉) — a minister of Wei who knew when to serve and when to withdraw (see 14.26). Confucius praises both models: the arrow (constant directness) and the scroll (strategic preservation). Both are forms of integrity. Connects to FUALL's framework: wisdom includes knowing when to act and when to preserve.

refashioned: The Master said, 'When someone can be spoken to and you do not speak, you lose the person. When someone cannot be spoken to and you speak anyway, you waste your words. The wise person wastes neither people nor words.'
elegant: Silence when speech is needed loses people. Speech when silence is needed wastes words. The wise lose neither.
distilled: Wisdom is knowing when to speak and when to stay silent — and never getting it backward.
notes: Shi ren (失人, losing a person) vs. Shi yan (失言, losing/wasting words). Zhi (智) here is social intelligence — reading situations correctly. Connects to FUALL's Agora: effective discourse requires discernment about audience and timing.

refashioned: The Master said, 'The truly committed scholar and the person of virtue will not preserve their lives at the expense of their principles. They will sacrifice their lives to keep their virtue intact.'
elegant: The virtuous do not purchase life by selling principle. They pay with life to preserve it.
distilled: There are things worth dying for, and the integrity of your principles is one of them.
notes: Sha shen cheng ren (殺身成仁) — 'kill the body to accomplish Ren.' One of the most demanding statements in the Analects. Confucius asserts that Ren (仁) is worth more than physical survival. This parallels Socrates' position at his trial. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom: the highest commitment is to principles that transcend individual survival.

refashioned: Zigong asked about the practice of virtue. The Master said, 'A craftsman who wants to do good work must first sharpen his tools. In whatever state you live, serve the worthiest of its great officers and befriend the most virtuous of its scholars.'
elegant: Sharpen your tools before the work: serve the worthiest officials, befriend the most virtuous scholars. That is how you practice virtue.
distilled: Virtue is practiced by choosing the best people to work for and the best people to learn from.
notes: Gong yu shan qi shi, bi xian li qi qi (工欲善其事，必先利其器) — 'The craftsman who would do good work must first sharpen his tools.' A famous Chinese proverb originating here. The 'tools' of virtue are relationships: good mentors and good friends. Connects to FUALL's framework: the quality of your intellectual community determines the quality of your development.

refashioned: Yan Yuan asked how a country should be governed. The Master said, 'Follow the Xia calendar. Use the Yin state carriage. Wear the Zhou ceremonial cap. Let the music be the Shao. Banish the songs of Zheng and keep away from smooth talkers. The songs of Zheng are licentious; smooth talkers are dangerous.'
elegant: Take the best from each dynasty: Xia's calendar, Yin's carriage, Zhou's cap, Shun's music. Banish licentious songs and slick speakers.
distilled: The best system takes the best practices from every era and guards against corruption in music and speech.
notes: Confucius constructs an ideal state by selecting the best institutional practices from the three dynasties: Xia (calendar/agriculture), Yin/Shang (state carriage/transportation), Zhou (ceremonial cap/ritual). The Shao (韶) music was created under the sage-king Shun. The songs of Zheng (鄭聲) were considered morally corrupting. Ning ren (佞人, specious/smooth talkers) — people who corrupt discourse. Connects to FUALL's framework: institutional design should preserve what works from every tradition while filtering out what corrupts.

refashioned: The Master said, 'If a person gives no thought to distant consequences, he will find trouble close at hand.'
elegant: Those who will not think ahead will soon find trouble at their door.
distilled: Neglecting the future guarantees that the future will arrive as a crisis.
notes: Ren wu yuan lü, bi you jin you (人無遠慮，必有近憂) — 'Without distant thinking, there must be near worries.' One of the most widely quoted Chinese proverbs. Connects to FUALL's Reason: foresight is a core component of wisdom.

refashioned: The Master said, 'It is hopeless. I have never met anyone who loves virtue the way people love beauty.'
elegant: I have never met a person who desires virtue the way he desires beauty. It is hopeless.
distilled: Humanity's failure is not that it cannot recognize virtue but that it does not desire virtue as strongly as it desires pleasure.
notes: Hao de ru hao se (好德如好色) — 'loving virtue as one loves beauty/sexual attractiveness.' Confucius's lament about the asymmetry between moral aspiration and natural desire. This is repeated from 9.18. Connects to FUALL's Reality: the human condition involves a persistent gap between what we know is right and what we naturally want.

refashioned: The Master said, 'Zang Wen was like a man who stole his own position. He knew the virtue and talent of Hui of Liuxia, yet failed to bring him into government alongside himself.'
elegant: He knew the best man for the job and left him out. Confucius said: that is stealing your own office.
distilled: Knowing who is best and failing to elevate them is a form of corruption equal to stealing the position yourself.
notes: Zang Wenzhong (臧文仲) — a minister of Lu. Hui of Liuxia (柳下惠) — a famous sage known for integrity. Zang is criticized not for incompetence but for failing to promote someone more capable. Qie wei (竊位, stealing a position) — occupying a role while preventing the most qualified from serving. Connects to FUALL's Guilds: the duty of good leadership is to identify and elevate talent.

refashioned: The Master said, 'Demand much from yourself and little from others, and you will keep resentment far away.'
elegant: Hard on yourself, easy on others — that is how you avoid resentment.
distilled: The person who demands more from himself than from others never becomes the object of bitterness.
notes: Gong zi hou er bo ze yu ren (躬自厚而薄責於人) — 'be thick in self-expectation and thin in blaming others.' A practical recipe for harmonious relationships. Connects to FUALL's Right: moral authority comes from holding yourself to higher standards than you impose on others.

refashioned: The Master said, 'If a person never asks himself, "What should I make of this? What should I make of this?" — then I truly can do nothing with him.'
elegant: The student who never asks 'What should I think about this?' is beyond teaching.
distilled: The unteachable person is not the one who lacks intelligence but the one who never questions.
notes: Ru zhi he, ru zhi he (如之何，如之何) — 'how about this, what about this.' The minimum requirement for learning is reflective questioning. Without it, no amount of teaching can take hold. Connects to FUALL's Academy: education fails not from lack of teachers but from lack of genuine inquiry in students.

refashioned: The Master said, 'When people spend the whole day together without their conversation ever touching on righteousness, and delight in showing off petty cleverness — theirs is a hopeless case.'
elegant: A whole day of talk without a word about what is right, filled instead with displays of small cunning — these people are beyond help.
distilled: People who never discuss what is right, only what is clever, have abandoned the only conversation that matters.
notes: Yi (義, righteousness) as the necessary content of meaningful discourse. Xiao hui (小慧, petty cleverness) as its substitute. The passage condemns discourse without moral substance. Connects to FUALL's Agora: public discourse that avoids questions of right and wrong degenerates into entertainment.

refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person considers righteousness essential in everything. He performs it through propriety, expresses it in humility, and completes it with faithfulness. This is what it means to be a superior person.'
elegant: Righteousness is the root. Propriety is the method. Humility is the manner. Faithfulness is the seal. That is the superior person.
distilled: The superior person's formula: righteousness as foundation, propriety as form, humility as style, faithfulness as proof.
notes: Yi (義) → Li (禮) → Sun (遜, humility) → Xin (信). Four stages of virtuous action: know what is right, do it properly, present it humbly, follow through faithfully. This is one of the most systematic descriptions of the Junzi in the Analects. Connects to FUALL's Right: virtue is not a single trait but an integrated practice.

refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person is troubled by his own lack of ability, not by others failing to recognize him.'
elegant: The superior person's worry: 'Am I good enough?' Not: 'Do they know how good I am?'
distilled: Focus on your competence, not your reputation.
notes: Bing wu neng (病無能, distressed by lack of ability). Another variation on the theme of 1.1, 1.16, 4.14, 14.32. The Junzi's orientation is always internal. Connects to FUALL's Academy: genuine learning is self-directed, not approval-seeking.

refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person dreads dying without having left a legacy.'
elegant: The superior person's deepest fear: dying without having left something worth remembering.
distilled: The real fear is not death but dying before your work matters.
notes: Ji mo wu wen (疾沒世而名不稱) — 'distressed at departing this world with no name.' This is not vanity but concern for legacy — the desire that one's life contribute something to the chain of transmission. Connects to FUALL's framework: the project's anxiety is that wisdom will not be transmitted.

refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person seeks what he needs within himself. The inferior person seeks it in others.'
elegant: Look within for what you need — the inferior person looks everywhere else.
distilled: Self-reliance in the search for virtue distinguishes the superior from the inferior person.
notes: Qiu zhu ji (求諸己) vs. Qiu zhu ren (求諸人). The Junzi's locus of control is internal; the Xiaoren's is external. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom: genuine understanding is developed internally, not borrowed from authority.

refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person is dignified without being contentious. He is sociable without being partisan.'
elegant: Dignified but not combative. Social but not factional. That is the superior person.
distilled: True character is firm without being aggressive and open without being tribal.
notes: Qun er bu dang (群而不黨) — 'associates but does not form factions.' The Junzi participates in community without becoming partisan. Connects to FUALL's Agora: productive discourse requires engagement without tribalism.

refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person does not promote someone merely because of their words, nor does he dismiss good words because of who said them.'
elegant: Do not elevate a person for speaking well. Do not reject wisdom because the speaker is flawed.
distilled: Judge arguments by their merit and people by their actions — never confuse the two.
notes: Bu yi yan ju ren, bu yi ren fei yan (不以言舉人，不以人廢言). Separate the evaluation of ideas from the evaluation of persons. This is a foundational principle of intellectual integrity. Connects to FUALL's Reason: truth is independent of its source.

refashioned: Zigong asked, 'Is there a single word that can serve as a guide for one's entire life?' The Master said, 'Perhaps the word "reciprocity." Do not do to others what you would not want done to yourself.'
elegant: One word for a lifetime: reciprocity. Do not inflict on others what you would refuse for yourself.
distilled: The single principle sufficient for a lifetime: do not do to others what you would not want done to you.
notes: Shu (恕, reciprocity) — Confucius's candidate for the one unifying ethical principle. Ji suo bu yu, wu shi yu ren (己所不欲，勿施於人) — the 'Silver Rule,' also in 12.2. The negative formulation ('do not do') is deliberate: it sets a floor rather than a ceiling, leaving room for individual judgment about positive action. This parallels the Golden Rule in Matthew 7:12 and Hillel's formulation in the Talmud. Connects to FUALL's Right: the minimum ethical principle is universally applicable.

refashioned: The Master said, 'In dealing with people, do I blame anyone too harshly or praise anyone too generously beyond what is warranted? If I do sometimes praise excessively, there is always a basis for it that I have verified. The common people today are the same people whose honesty gave the three dynasties their straight path.'
elegant: If I praise, there is evidence. If I blame, there is cause. The people who walk the earth today are the same honest stock that sustained three dynasties.
distilled: Judgment must be grounded in evidence, and faith in ordinary people is justified by the track record of civilization.
notes: The 'three dynasties' (san dai, 三代) — Xia, Shang, Zhou — are the Confucian golden age of righteous governance. Confucius grounds his faith in human potential in historical evidence: these same people sustained great civilizations. Connects to FUALL's framework: the project's faith in human capacity is not naive but historically informed.

refashioned: The Master said, 'In my younger days, a historian would leave a blank where he was uncertain, and a man who owned a horse would lend it to another. Now such things no longer exist.'
elegant: Once, historians admitted uncertainty and horse-owners shared. Now neither happens.
distilled: The decline of intellectual honesty and personal generosity are symptoms of the same civilizational erosion.
notes: Two seemingly unrelated examples linked by a common thread: declining integrity. The historian's blank space (que wen, 闕文) represents intellectual honesty — admitting what you do not know. The horse-lending represents generosity and trust. Both have disappeared. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: the degradation of character shows up in both intellectual and social life simultaneously.

refashioned: The Master said, 'Clever-sounding words corrupt virtue. Lack of patience in small matters ruins great plans.'
elegant: Clever words corrupt virtue. Impatience with small things destroys great ones.
distilled: Two destroyers of excellence: persuasive nonsense and impatience with detail.
notes: Qiao yan luan de (巧言亂德) — see 1.3, 17.17. Xiao bu ren ze luan da mou (小不忍則亂大謀) — 'inability to endure small things disrupts great plans.' Two distinct warnings: one about discourse (bad speech corrupts character), one about temperament (impatience destroys strategy). Connects to FUALL's Reason and Right: both require discipline against seductive shortcuts.

refashioned: The Master said, 'When the crowd hates someone, you must examine the case yourself. When the crowd loves someone, you must also examine the case yourself.'
elegant: The crowd's hatred requires investigation. The crowd's love requires it equally.
distilled: Popular opinion — whether for or against — is never a substitute for independent judgment.
notes: Zhong wu zhi, bi cha yan; zhong hao zhi, bi cha yan (眾惡之，必察焉；眾好之，必察焉). Confucius demands independent verification against mob sentiment in both directions. This echoes 13.24. Connects to FUALL's Reason: truth is not determined by consensus.

refashioned: The Master said, 'A person can enlarge the principles he follows. But principles do not enlarge the person.'
elegant: You can make the Way greater. The Way cannot make you greater — that is your work alone.
distilled: The Dao does not elevate you; you elevate the Dao through how you live it.
notes: Ren neng hong dao, fei dao hong ren (人能弘道，非道弘人) — 'people can enlarge the Way; the Way does not enlarge people.' Confucius insists on human agency: the Dao is not a force that automatically improves those who follow it; it requires active human effort to be realized. Connects to FUALL's framework: wisdom is achieved through human effort, not received passively from tradition.

refashioned: The Master said, 'To have faults and not correct them — that is the real fault.'
elegant: The fault is not in failing. The fault is in refusing to fix it.
distilled: An uncorrected fault is the only real fault.
notes: Guo er bu gai, shi wei guo yi (過而不改，是謂過矣). One of the most quoted lines from the Analects. The distinction between error (inevitable) and persistence in error (a moral failing). Connects to FUALL's framework: self-correction is the engine of all improvement.

refashioned: The Master said, 'I once went the whole day without eating and the whole night without sleeping, occupied with thinking. It was useless. Better to learn.'
elegant: A day without food, a night without sleep, all spent thinking — and nothing gained. Better to learn from others.
distilled: Pure thought without input is sterile; learning must feed reflection.
notes: Si vs. Xue (思 vs. 學) — thinking vs. learning. This complements 2.15: 'Learning without thought is labor lost; thought without learning is perilous.' Together they establish Confucius's epistemology: both input (learning) and processing (thought) are necessary; neither alone is sufficient. Connects to FUALL's Academy: genuine understanding requires both study and reflection.

refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person's object is truth, not food. Farming may lead to hunger; learning may lead to salary. But the superior person worries about truth, not about poverty.'
elegant: The superior person pursues truth, not livelihood. Poverty may come; truth must not be lost.
distilled: Better to be poor and oriented toward truth than comfortable and oriented toward survival.
notes: Mou dao bu mou shi (謀道不謀食) — 'plan for the Way, not for food.' The Junzi's priority hierarchy: Dao above material security. This does not mean Confucius was indifferent to poverty (see 13.9: 'enrich them, then teach them') but that the scholar's personal orientation must be toward truth. Connects to FUALL's Academy: the intellectual vocation requires accepting material uncertainty.

refashioned: The Master said, 'When knowledge is sufficient to attain a position but virtue is insufficient to hold it, you will lose whatever you gain. When knowledge and virtue are both sufficient, but you cannot govern with dignity, the people will not respect you. When you have knowledge, virtue, and dignity, but try to move the people in ways that violate propriety, full excellence is still not reached.'
elegant: Knowledge gains. Virtue holds. Dignity commands respect. Propriety completes the work. Miss any one and excellence falls short.
distilled: Mastery requires four things in sequence: knowledge to attain, virtue to hold, dignity to command, and propriety to complete.
notes: A four-stage model of leadership excellence: Zhi (知, knowledge) → De (德, virtue) → Zhuang (莊, dignity) → Li (禮, propriety). Each is necessary but insufficient alone. The sequence matters: you can have all three prior qualities and still fail at the final step. Connects to FUALL's framework: complete leadership integrates intellectual, moral, social, and cultural competence.

refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person cannot be fully known from small tasks, but can be entrusted with great responsibilities. The inferior person cannot be entrusted with great responsibilities, but can be observed in small tasks.'
elegant: You cannot judge a great person by small tasks, nor entrust a small person with great ones. Scale reveals character.
distilled: Great people are tested by great responsibilities; small people are exposed by them.
notes: Junzi bu ke xiao zhi, er ke da shou (君子不可小知，而可大受). The principle: scope of assessment must match scope of capability. Small tasks may not reveal a great person's strengths; great tasks will certainly reveal a small person's limitations. Connects to FUALL's Guilds: matching assessment scale to capability level.

refashioned: The Master said, 'Virtue is more essential to people than water or fire. I have seen people die from stepping into water or fire, but I have never seen anyone die from stepping into the path of virtue.'
elegant: Water and fire can kill you. Virtue never has. Yet people fear virtue more than either.
distilled: People fear the demands of virtue more than physical danger, even though virtue has never destroyed anyone.
notes: De (virtue) compared to the basic necessities (water and fire). Confucius argues that virtue is both more essential and less dangerous than material necessities, yet people resist it more. The paradox: we fear what cannot harm us and embrace what can.

refashioned: The Master said, 'Let every person consider virtue their own personal responsibility. In this matter, you need not defer even to your teacher.'
elegant: In the matter of virtue, do not yield — not even to your teacher.
distilled: Virtue is the one domain where deference to authority is not a virtue.
notes: Dang ren bu rang yu shi (當仁不讓於師) — 'when it comes to Ren, do not yield even to your teacher.' A striking statement from a teacher: in the pursuit of virtue, the student's own responsibility is primary. No authority figure can substitute for personal moral agency. Connects to FUALL's framework: wisdom must ultimately be individually owned, not borrowed from institutions.

refashioned: The Master said, 'The superior person is firm in principle, not merely stubborn.'
elegant: Principled firmness is not the same as stubbornness. One has a reason; the other has only rigidity.
distilled: True firmness is loyalty to principle; mere stubbornness is loyalty to position.
notes: Zhen er bu liang (貞而不諒) — 'upright but not inflexibly loyal to a single position.' The Junzi's firmness is in values (Yi, 義), not in arbitrary positions. Connects to FUALL's Reason: genuine conviction is flexible in method but fixed in principle.

refashioned: The Master said, 'In serving his ruler, a minister attends reverently to his duties and treats his salary as secondary.'
elegant: Serve with reverence. Let compensation be an afterthought.
distilled: The public servant's priority is duty, not pay.
notes: Shi jun, jing qi shi er hou qi shi (事君，敬其事而後其食). Restates the theme of 14.1: service must be motivated by duty, not by salary. Connects to FUALL's framework: institutional health depends on people oriented toward mission rather than compensation.

refashioned: The Master said, 'In education, there should be no class distinctions.'
elegant: Teach everyone. No exceptions. No classes.
distilled: Education should be universal, not reserved for any social class.
notes: You jiao wu lei (有教無類) — 'in teaching, there are no categories.' One of Confucius's most revolutionary principles. In his era, education was restricted to the aristocracy. Confucius accepted students from all backgrounds, charging only a token fee (a bundle of dried meat). This is the foundation of FUALL's educational philosophy: the Academy must be open to all.

refashioned: The Master said, 'Those who follow different paths cannot make plans together.'
elegant: If your ways diverge, your plans cannot converge.
distilled: People who disagree on fundamentals cannot collaborate on specifics.
notes: Dao bu tong, bu xiang wei mou (道不同，不相為謀). The Dao (道, Way) here refers to fundamental values and direction, not trivial preferences. Cooperation requires shared foundational commitments. Connects to FUALL's framework: the project's institutions require shared commitment to Wisdom as a precondition for productive work.

refashioned: The Master said, 'In language, it is simply required that it convey the meaning.'
elegant: Language has one job: to convey meaning. Everything else is ornament.
distilled: The purpose of language is clarity, not decoration.
notes: Ci da er yi yi (辭達而已矣) — 'words arrive at meaning and that is all.' This is not anti-literary but anti-ornamental: language should serve communication, not display. Connects to FUALL's Reason: clear thinking requires clear expression.

refashioned: When the blind Music-master Mian came to visit, the Master guided him at every step. 'Here are the stairs. Here is the mat. So-and-so is seated here; so-and-so there.' After Mian left, Zizhang asked, 'Is this the proper way to assist a music-master?' The Master said, 'Yes. This is how one guides those who cannot see.'
elegant: Confucius guided the blind musician step by step — stairs, mat, who sat where. 'Is this the rule?' his student asked. 'Yes. This is how you help those who cannot see.'
distilled: True courtesy is not ceremony but practical helpfulness — telling people what they need to know in the moment they need it.
notes: Music-master Mian (師冕) — court musicians were traditionally blind. Confucius's detailed guidance (stairs, mat, seating) is an example of Li (禮) as practical compassion, not empty formality. The passage illustrates that propriety at its best is attentive care for others' actual needs. Connects to FUALL's Right: ritual is the mechanism through which care becomes action.

refashioned:
The Ji family planned to attack Zhuanyu. Ran You and Zilu met with Confucius and said, 'Our chief is about to take military action against Zhuanyu.' Confucius said, 'Qiu, is this not your fault? A former king appointed Zhuanyu's ruler to preside over sacrifices. It lies within our own state. Its ruler is a minister connected to the sovereign. What business has your chief attacking it?'

Ran You said, 'Our master wants it — neither of us ministers supports it.' Confucius replied, 'Qiu! There is an old saying: "When you can serve, take your place; when you cannot, step down." How can a guide who fails to support the blind when they stumble be of any use? And further — you speak wrongly. When a tiger escapes its cage or jade is broken in its box, whose fault is it? The keeper's.

Ran You said, 'But Zhuanyu is strong and close to Pi. If we don't take it now, it will trouble our descendants.' Confucius said, 'Qiu! The superior person detests those who refuse to say "I want this" and instead invent justifications.

I have heard that rulers should worry not about having too few people but about inequality; not about poverty but about discontent. When people have their proper places, there is no poverty. When there is harmony, there is no shortage of people. When people are content, there is no rebellion.

If distant peoples will not submit, attract them through culture and virtue. Once they come, keep them content. But you two are helping your chief, and the distant peoples will not come, and the state itself is fracturing — and yet he plans war within his own borders! I fear the Ji family's trouble will come not from Zhuanyu but from within their own walls.'
elegant: The tiger has escaped the cage. The jade is shattered in its box. And you ask who is to blame? — The keeper. The Ji family's real enemy is not across the border but behind its own walls.
distilled: Rulers who manufacture external threats to distract from internal decay will find the decay was the real threat all along.
notes: One of the longest and most politically substantive passages in the Analects. Zhuanyu (顓臾) — a small dependent state within Lu's territory. The Ji family (季氏) — the most powerful of the three families controlling Lu. Confucius's argument against the attack is multi-layered: legal (Zhuanyu has legitimate status), moral (the Ji ministers are complicit), strategic (internal division is the real danger). The maxim 'bu huan gua er huan bu jun' (不患寡而患不均, worry not about scarcity but about inequality) became a foundational Chinese political principle. Connects to FUALL's domains of power: when social power (the Ji family) overrides legitimate authority (the Duke), the system is already failing.

refashioned:
Confucius said, 'When the realm is well-governed, ceremonies, music, and military campaigns originate from the sovereign. When it is badly governed, they originate from the regional lords. When lords control these things, power is typically lost within ten generations. When their ministers control them, within five. When their ministers' subordinates hold power, within three.

When right principles prevail, government does not fall into the hands of great officers. When right principles prevail, the common people have nothing to dispute about.'
elegant: Power that originates from the sovereign lasts ten generations. From the nobles, five. From their subordinates, three. The further power drifts from its source, the faster it decays.
distilled: The more dispersed legitimate authority becomes from its proper source, the faster the system collapses.
notes: A theory of political entropy: legitimate authority degrades as it passes to lower levels. The progression (son of Heaven → princes → great officers → subsidiary ministers) describes the historical decay of the Zhou dynasty. Each transfer of power halves the time before collapse. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: institutional legitimacy decays when authority migrates from its proper locus.

refashioned: Confucius said, 'Revenue has left the ducal house for five generations now. Government has been in the hands of the great officers for four. This is why the descendants of the three Huan families are in decline.'
elegant: Five generations since the duke lost revenue. Four since he lost governance. The great families who seized power are now declining in turn.
distilled: Usurped power decays just as the legitimate power it replaced did.
notes: The three Huan families (三桓) — Ji, Meng, and Shu — descendants of Duke Huan of Lu, who controlled the state. Confucius observes that the usurpers are themselves decaying, illustrating the principle from 16.2: illegitimate power is inherently unstable. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: the rot of institutional capture is self-limiting — the captors eventually decay too.

refashioned: Confucius said, 'Three friendships benefit you, and three harm you. Friendship with the upright, the sincere, and the well-informed benefits you. Friendship with the flattering, the two-faced, and the glib-tongued harms you.'
elegant: Beneficial friends: the honest, the sincere, the well-read. Harmful friends: the flattering, the slippery, the smooth-talking.
distilled: Your friends shape your character; choose the honest over the pleasant.
notes: San yi you, san sun you (三益友，三損友). The taxonomy of friendship is based not on personal warmth but on moral and intellectual quality. Zhi (直, upright), Liang (諒, sincere), and Duo wen (多聞, well-informed) are the beneficial types. Pian pi (便辟, specious), Shan rou (善柔, insinuatingly soft), and Pian ning (便佞, glib) are the harmful types. Connects to FUALL's Agora: the quality of your discourse community determines the quality of your thinking.

refashioned: Confucius said, 'Three pleasures benefit you, and three harm you. Finding pleasure in studying ritual and music, in speaking of others' goodness, and in having many worthy friends benefits you. Finding pleasure in extravagance, in idleness, and in feasting harms you.'
elegant: Beneficial pleasures: learning, praising goodness, worthy friends. Harmful pleasures: luxury, idleness, feasting.
distilled: The pleasures that build you up are learning, generosity, and good company; the ones that break you down are indulgence, laziness, and excess.
notes: San yi le, san sun le (三益樂，三損樂). The taxonomy of pleasure parallels the taxonomy of friendship in 16.4. Confucius does not condemn pleasure itself but distinguishes constructive from destructive forms. Li yue (禮樂, ritual and music) as beneficial pleasure connects learning to joy. Connects to FUALL's framework: the educational institutions should be sources of genuine pleasure, not mere obligation.

refashioned: Confucius said, 'Three errors are common when in the presence of a person of authority. Speaking before it is your turn — that is rashness. Not speaking when it is your turn — that is concealment. Speaking without reading the other person's expression — that is blindness.'
elegant: Three errors in the presence of the powerful: speaking too soon (rashness), not speaking at all (concealment), speaking without reading the room (blindness).
distilled: In the presence of authority, the three failures are premature speech, withheld speech, and tone-deaf speech.
notes: Zao (躁, rashness), Yin (隱, concealment), Gu (瞽, blindness). These are errors of timing and social awareness, not of content. All three relate to Zhi (智, wisdom) as social intelligence — knowing when and how to speak. Connects to FUALL's Agora: effective discourse requires not just having something to say but knowing when and how to say it.

refashioned: Confucius said, 'The superior person guards against three things. In youth, when his energies are unsettled, he guards against lust. In his prime, when his energies are at full strength, he guards against combativeness. In old age, when his energies are declining, he guards against acquisitiveness.'
elegant: Youth: guard against lust. Prime: guard against aggression. Old age: guard against greed. Each stage has its specific danger.
distilled: The dangers shift with age — desire, then dominance, then accumulation — and each requires its own vigilance.
notes: San jie (三戒, three warnings). The developmental psychology is practical: each life stage presents a characteristic temptation tied to physical energy levels. Se (色, lust/desire), Dou (鬥, combativeness), De (得, acquisitiveness). Connects to FUALL's framework: self-knowledge must include awareness of how your vulnerabilities change over time.

refashioned: Confucius said, 'The superior person stands in awe of three things: the will of Heaven, great people, and the words of sages. The inferior person, not knowing Heaven's will, does not stand in awe of it. He is disrespectful to great people and mocks the words of sages.'
elegant: The superior person reveres Heaven's will, great people, and the words of sages. The inferior person, knowing none of these, fears none of them.
distilled: The capacity for awe distinguishes the wise from the foolish; those who revere nothing understand nothing.
notes: San wei (三畏, three objects of awe). Tian ming (天命, Heaven's mandate/ordinances) — the moral order of the universe. Da ren (大人, great people) — those who embody the Dao. Sheng ren zhi yan (聖人之言, words of sages) — the accumulated wisdom tradition. The Xiaoren's failure is not rebellion but ignorance: he does not know these things exist, so he cannot respect them. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom: genuine understanding begins with recognition that some things are greater than oneself.

refashioned: Confucius said, 'Those born with knowledge are the highest class. Those who learn and acquire knowledge are next. Those who are dull but persist in learning come after them. Those who are dull and refuse to learn — they are the lowest.'
elegant: Born knowing: highest. Learning to know: next. Struggling to learn: honorable. Refusing to learn: lowest.
distilled: The ranking of people is not by natural intelligence but by willingness to learn.
notes: Sheng er zhi zhi (生而知之) — 'born knowing.' Confucius believed such people existed but were extremely rare (he did not claim to be one — see 7.20). The hierarchy's real message is that effort (xue, 學) matters more than innate capacity. The lowest class is defined not by stupidity but by refusal to learn. Connects to FUALL's Academy: the only disqualifying trait is the refusal to engage.

refashioned: Confucius said, 'The superior person gives thoughtful attention to nine things: In seeing, he aims for clarity. In hearing, for accuracy. In expression, for warmth. In manner, for respect. In speech, for sincerity. In work, for reverent care. In doubt, for questioning others. In anger, for considering the consequences. When seeing a chance for gain, for thinking of what is right.'
elegant: Nine disciplines: clear sight, sharp hearing, warm expression, respectful manner, sincere speech, careful work, honest questioning, tempered anger, righteous restraint before gain.
distilled: The superior person applies disciplined attention to every faculty: eyes, ears, face, body, tongue, hands, doubts, anger, and desire.
notes: Jiu si (九思, nine subjects of thoughtful consideration). This is one of the most comprehensive self-cultivation checklists in the Analects. Each pairs a human faculty or situation with its proper discipline. The ninth — yi yi (義, righteousness when seeing gain) — echoes 14.13 and 19.1. Connects to FUALL's Wisdom: each dimension of human capacity requires its own form of disciplined attention.

refashioned: Confucius said, 'Pursuing good as if you could never reach it, shrinking from evil as from boiling water — I have seen such people and heard such words. Living in seclusion to examine one's aims, practicing righteousness to fulfill one's principles — I have heard such words, but I have never seen such people.'
elegant: Chasing good as if it were always out of reach, recoiling from evil as from boiling water — such people exist. But those who live in quiet seclusion practicing what they preach? I have heard of them. I have never met one.
distilled: People who flee evil are common enough; people who quietly practice righteousness in seclusion are almost mythical.
notes: The asymmetry: moral urgency (pursuit of good, avoidance of evil) is observable; consistent private practice of principles is almost never seen. Confucius's skepticism about recluses (yin ju, 隱居) who claim to practice righteousness in isolation — he has 'heard' the claim but 'never seen' it verified. Connects to FUALL's framework: virtue must be publicly demonstrated, not merely privately claimed.

refashioned: Duke Jing of Qi had a thousand teams of horses, but when he died, no one praised him for a single virtue. Bo Yi and Shu Qi starved to death on Mount Shouyang, and people praise them to this day. Is this not what the saying means?
elegant: A duke with four thousand horses: no one praised his virtue. Two men who starved on a mountain: praised for millennia. Wealth leaves no legacy; character does.
distilled: Wealth is forgotten at death; virtue is remembered across centuries.
notes: Duke Jing of Qi (齊景公) — see 12.11, 18.3. Bo Yi (伯夷) and Shu Qi (叔齊) — brothers who refused to serve the Zhou dynasty after it overthrew the Shang, and starved rather than eat Zhou grain on Mount Shouyang. They are Confucian exemplars of principled refusal. The 'saying' referenced is likely from 12.10 (about virtue vs. wealth). Connects to FUALL's framework: legacy is determined by character, not by resources.

refashioned: Chen Kang asked Confucius's son Boyu, 'Have you received any special instruction from your father that we haven't?' Boyu replied, 'No. Once, when he was standing alone and I hurried past, he asked, "Have you studied the Odes?" I said not yet. He said, "If you don't study the Odes, you will have nothing to speak with." So I went and studied the Odes. Another time, he asked, "Have you studied the rules of Propriety?" I said not yet. He said, "If you don't study Propriety, your character cannot be established." So I went and studied Propriety. I have heard only these two lessons from him.' Chen Kang went away delighted, saying, 'I asked one question and got three answers: about the Odes, about Propriety, and about how a father keeps a proper distance from his son.'
elegant: Confucius's son received no special teaching — just two commands: study the Odes, study Propriety. A visitor asked one question and learned three things: the value of poetry, the value of ritual, and that a sage keeps his own son at arm's length.
distilled: The sage's son received no privilege — only the same two disciplines everyone needs: poetry and propriety.
notes: Boyu (伯魚, Kong Li) — Confucius's son. Chen Kang (陳亢, also Chen Ziqin) — a disciple. The two pillars of Confucian education: Shi (詩, the Odes/Poetry) for cultivating speech and emotional intelligence, and Li (禮, Propriety) for establishing character. The 'three things' (san yi, 三益): Odes, Propriety, and the principle that a father should not show favoritism to his own son in teaching. Connects to FUALL's Academy: education must be universal and impartial, even within the teacher's own family.

refashioned: The wife of a state's ruler is called 'Fu Ren' by her husband. She calls herself 'Xiao Tong.' The people call her 'Jun Fu Ren.' To people of other states, she is called 'Gua Xiao Jun.' People of other states also call her 'Jun Fu Ren.'
elegant: A ruler's wife has five names depending on who speaks and to whom — each title precisely calibrated to relationship and context.
distilled: Proper naming reflects proper relationships; every title carries a specific social meaning.
notes: A passage about Zhengming (正名, rectification of names) applied to court protocol. The different titles for the same person (Fu Ren, Xiao Tong, Jun Fu Ren, Gua Xiao Jun) encode relationships of authority, humility, and diplomatic courtesy. This is Li (禮) at its most formal.

refashioned: Yang Huo wanted to see Confucius, but Confucius would not visit him. So Yang Huo sent a pig as a gift, knowing protocol required a return visit. Confucius chose a time when Yang Huo was out to make his reciprocal call — but met him on the road. Yang Huo said, 'Come, I want to speak with you. Can a man who hoards his talent while his country falls apart be called benevolent? No. Can a man who wants to serve but keeps missing his chance be called wise? No. Time is passing. The years will not wait.' Confucius said, 'Very well. I will take office.'
elegant: Yang Huo trapped Confucius with a gift and a question: 'Can a man who lets his country fall apart be called benevolent?' Confucius conceded: 'I will serve.'
distilled: Even the sage can be cornered by his own principles: if you believe in service, you cannot forever refuse to serve.
notes: Yang Huo (陽貨, also Yang Hu) — a powerful and controversial steward of the Ji family who effectively seized control of Lu around 505 BCE. This passage is rich with irony: Yang Huo uses Confucius's own values (Ren, Zhi) against him. The pig-gift creates a ritual obligation. Confucius's 'I will take office' is likely diplomatic rather than sincere — he never actually served Yang Huo. Connects to FUALL's framework: Li (禮) can be weaponized, creating obligations the recipient cannot refuse without violating his own principles.

refashioned: The Master said, 'By nature, people are similar. Through practice and habit, they grow far apart.'
elegant: Nature makes us alike. Habit makes us different.
distilled: Human beings are born similar; it is practice that creates the vast differences between them.
notes: Xing xiang jin ye, xi xiang yuan ye (性相近也，習相遠也). One of the most influential statements in Chinese philosophy. Xing (性, nature) is the starting point; Xi (習, practice/habit) is the differentiating force. This is the Confucian case for education: since nature is roughly equal, differences are produced by cultivation and environment. Connects directly to FUALL's framework: the educational institutions exist because human potential is shaped by practice, not predetermined by nature.

refashioned: The Master said, 'Only the wisest and the most foolish never change.'
elegant: The highest wisdom and the deepest foolishness have one thing in common: neither can be moved.
distilled: Only the very best and the very worst are beyond the reach of education.
notes: Wei shang zhi yu xia yu bu yi (唯上知與下愚不移). A qualification of 17.2: while most people are shaped by practice, the extreme ends are fixed. This sets the boundaries of education's reach: it works for the vast middle but cannot elevate the utterly closed or further enlighten the already complete.

refashioned: When the Master arrived in Wucheng and heard the sound of stringed instruments and singing, he smiled and said, 'Why use an ox-cleaver to kill a chicken?' Ziyu replied, 'Master, I once heard you say: "When the educated person learns the Way, he loves others. When the common person learns the Way, he is easy to govern."' The Master said, 'My students, Ziyu is right. I was joking.'
elegant: Confucius teased his student for teaching music in a small town. The student quoted the Master's own words back at him. Confucius laughed: 'You are right. I was joking.'
distilled: The teacher who mocks the application of his own principles in small places has forgotten that principles have no minimum scale.
notes: Wucheng (武城) — a small district governed by Ziyu (子游, Yan Yan). The 'ox-cleaver for a chicken' (ge ji yan yong niu dao, 割雞焉用牛刀) became a famous Chinese idiom for using excessive means. Ziyu's response is sharp: the Dao applies everywhere, regardless of scale. Confucius's gracious retreat demonstrates intellectual honesty. Connects to FUALL's framework: the educational mission applies to every community, not just the major centers.

refashioned: Gongshan Furao, who held the city of Pi in rebellion, invited Confucius to join him. The Master was inclined to go. Zilu was displeased: 'You cannot go! Why would you even consider visiting Gongshan?' The Master said, 'Would he invite me without reason? If anyone employs me, I could create an Eastern Zhou.'
elegant: A rebel invited him. Confucius was tempted: 'If anyone employs me, I could rebuild the Zhou dynasty here in the east.'
distilled: The reformer's temptation: any platform, even a rebel's, might serve the cause.
notes: Gongshan Furao (公山弗擾) — a retainer of the Ji family who seized Pi in rebellion (~502 BCE). Confucius's willingness to consider the invitation reveals his desperate desire for a political platform. 'Eastern Zhou' (dong Zhou, 東周) — Confucius dreamed of restoring the golden age of early Zhou governance. Zilu's objection is practical: you cannot build legitimate governance on a rebel's foundation. Connects to FUALL's framework: the tension between pragmatic opportunity and principled restraint.

refashioned: Zizhang asked about perfect virtue. Confucius said, 'To practice five things everywhere under heaven constitutes perfect virtue.' Zizhang asked what they were. 'Gravity, generosity, sincerity, earnestness, and kindness. Gravity prevents disrespect. Generosity wins people over. Sincerity earns trust. Earnestness produces results. Kindness enables you to employ others.'
elegant: Five universals of virtue: gravity earns respect, generosity wins loyalty, sincerity earns trust, earnestness achieves results, kindness enables service.
distilled: Perfect virtue is five things practiced everywhere: be serious, generous, honest, diligent, and kind.
notes: Wu mei (五美): Gong (恭, gravity), Kuan (寬, generosity), Xin (信, sincerity), Min (敏, earnestness), Hui (惠, kindness). Each virtue is paired with its practical benefit. This is Ren (仁) broken down into operational components. Connects to FUALL's Right: virtue is not abstract but consists of specific, practicable qualities.

refashioned: Pi Xi, holding Zhongmou in rebellion, invited the Master to visit. The Master was inclined to go. Zilu objected: 'Master, you once said that the superior person will not associate with one who commits evil in his own person. Pi Xi holds Zhongmou in rebellion — how can you go?' The Master said, 'Yes, I said that. But is it not also said: what is truly hard cannot be worn thin by grinding? What is truly white cannot be stained by dyeing? Am I a bitter gourd, meant to hang on the wall and never be eaten?'
elegant: 'Am I a bitter gourd, hung on the wall, never to be tasted?' The Master longed to be used — even by imperfect employers.
distilled: A talent that refuses every imperfect opportunity becomes an ornament rather than an instrument.
notes: Pi Xi (佛肸) — a rebel holding Zhongmou in the state of Jin. Confucius uses two metaphors: the ungrindable stone (moral resilience) and the bitter gourd (the waste of unused talent). His frustration is existential: he cannot find a ruler worthy of his principles, yet refusing all opportunities means his teaching goes unimplemented. Connects to 14.41: knowing it's hopeless and trying anyway.

refashioned:
The Master said, 'Yu, have you heard of the six virtues and their six corruptions?' Zilu said he had not. 'Sit down and I will tell you.

Love of benevolence without love of learning leads to foolish credulity. Love of knowledge without love of learning leads to aimless speculation. Love of honesty without love of learning leads to hurtful bluntness. Love of directness without love of learning leads to rudeness. Love of courage without love of learning leads to recklessness. Love of firmness without love of learning leads to rash extremism.'
elegant: Every virtue, without learning, becomes its own corruption: benevolence becomes naivety, knowledge becomes distraction, honesty becomes cruelty, directness becomes rudeness, courage becomes recklessness, firmness becomes fanaticism.
distilled: Every virtue has a shadow, and learning is the only thing that keeps virtue from becoming vice.
notes: Liu yan liu bi (六言六蔽, six words and six becloudings). This is one of the most psychologically sophisticated passages in the Analects. Each virtue (Ren 仁, Zhi 知, Xin 信, Zhi 直, Yong 勇, Gang 剛) becomes destructive without the discipline of Xue (學, learning). Learning is not a virtue in itself but the corrective that keeps all virtues calibrated. Connects directly to FUALL's framework: Wisdom requires all three components (Reality, Reason, Right) — any one without the others produces distortion.

refashioned: The Master said, 'My students, why do you not study the Book of Poetry? The Odes stimulate the mind. They can be used for self-reflection. They teach sociability. They show how to express resentment properly. From them you learn the immediate duty to your father and the broader duty to your ruler. You also learn the names of many birds, beasts, and plants.'
elegant: Study poetry: it awakens, reflects, socializes, channels anger, teaches duty, and names the world. No other text does so many things at once.
distilled: Poetry does six things no other study can: it stimulates, mirrors, connects, disciplines emotion, teaches duty, and names the world.
notes: Shi ke yi xing (詩可以興) — the Shijing (詩經, Book of Poetry/Odes) as the foundation of education. The six functions: Xing (興, stimulate), Guan (觀, observe/reflect), Qun (群, socialize), Yuan (怨, express grievance properly), Shi (事, serve father and prince), and Zhi (知, know the natural world). This is Confucius's theory of liberal education: the humanities are not ornamental but foundational. Connects to FUALL's Academy: literature is the primary vehicle for emotional, social, and intellectual development.

refashioned: The Master said to his son Boyu, 'Have you studied the first sections of the Odes — the Zhounan and Shaonan? A person who has not studied them is like someone standing with his face against a wall — he can see nothing.'
elegant: Without the Odes, you are staring at a wall. You can see nothing.
distilled: A person uneducated in the foundational texts is blind to the world.
notes: Zhounan (周南) and Shaonan (召南) — the first two sections of the Shijing, dealing with domestic life, marriage, and proper conduct. Boyu (伯魚) — Confucius's son (see 16.13). The wall metaphor is vivid: without literary education, you have no perspective, no depth, no angle of vision.

refashioned: The Master said, 'They keep saying "propriety, propriety" — but is propriety just a matter of jade and silk? They keep saying "music, music" — but is music just bells and drums?'
elegant: Propriety is not jade and silk. Music is not bells and drums. Both are far deeper than their outward forms.
distilled: Ritual and music are not their physical trappings; reducing them to objects is missing the point entirely.
notes: Li (禮) and Yue (樂) — the two pillars of Confucian culture. Confucius protests against reducing them to material forms (jade, silk, bells, drums). The inner meaning of Li is right relationship; the inner meaning of Yue is harmony. When people equate these with their physical instruments, the substance is lost. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: when institutions are reduced to their outward forms, their purpose has already been forgotten.

refashioned: The Master said, 'A person who projects stern firmness while being inwardly weak is like a petty thief — one who breaks through walls or climbs over fences.'
elegant: Outward toughness hiding inner weakness is not leadership — it is burglary.
distilled: Projecting strength to conceal weakness is not virtue but a form of theft.
notes: Se li nei ren (色厲內荏, stern outside, weak inside). The comparison to a thief (chuan yu, 穿窬, one who tunnels or climbs walls) is deliberately degrading: the person who performs strength while lacking it is committing a kind of fraud. Connects to FUALL's framework: authenticity is foundational — performance without substance is a form of deception.

refashioned: The Master said, 'The carefully agreeable people of the villages are the thieves of virtue.'
elegant: The village conformists who offend no one and stand for nothing — they are virtue's pickpockets.
distilled: The person who is pleasant to everyone and principled about nothing is the most dangerous enemy of virtue.
notes: Xiang yuan, de zhi zei ye (鄉原，德之賊也) — 'the village worthy is the thief of virtue.' One of Confucius's sharpest sayings. The xiang yuan (鄉原/鄉愿) is the person who goes along with everyone, appears moral, but actually has no convictions. Mencius later elaborated this concept extensively (Mencius 7B.37). Connects to FUALL's framework: the most dangerous threat to moral seriousness is not open vice but agreeable emptiness.

refashioned: The Master said, 'To repeat what you hear on the road as you walk along is to throw away your virtue.'
elegant: Gossip is virtue's hemorrhage — it bleeds out as you walk.
distilled: Repeating unverified information as you go is a steady erosion of your moral credibility.
notes: Dao ting tu shuo (道聽途說) — 'heard on the road, told on the path.' This became a standard Chinese idiom for spreading rumors and gossip. De zhi qi (德之棄, casting away virtue). Unreflective repetition of hearsay damages both truth and character. Connects to FUALL's Reason: information must be verified before transmission.

refashioned: The Master said, 'What contemptible creatures! How impossible it is to serve one's ruler alongside them! Before they have what they want, they are consumed with getting it. Once they have it, they are consumed with fear of losing it. And when they fear losing it, there is nothing they will not do.'
elegant: Before they get power, they scheme to get it. After they get it, they scheme to keep it. Between the two, there is nothing they won't do.
distilled: People driven by acquisition and fear of loss will commit any act to satisfy either impulse.
notes: Bi fu (鄙夫, mean/contemptible person). The psychological portrait: anxiety about getting and anxiety about losing produce identical moral degradation. The bi fu cannot serve alongside a ruler because his self-interest will override any public duty. Connects to FUALL's domains of power: when personal interest captures political roles, governance collapses.

refashioned: The Master said, 'In the old days, people had three characteristic failings, which may no longer exist in their original form. Ancient high-mindedness meant disregarding petty things — today's version is wild license. Ancient dignity meant grave reserve — today's version is quarrelsome pettiness. Ancient simplicity meant straightforwardness — today's version is outright deceit.'
elegant: Ancient boldness ignored trifles; modern boldness ignores decency. Ancient gravity was reserved; modern gravity is quarrelsome. Ancient simplicity was direct; modern simplicity is dishonest.
distilled: Every generation degrades the virtues it inherits: boldness becomes recklessness, dignity becomes aggression, simplicity becomes deceit.
notes: Confucius describes a three-part moral decay where each classical virtue has degenerated into a parody of itself. Kuang (狂, high-mindedness) → dang (蕩, license). Jin (矜, dignity) → jiao (忿戾, quarrelsome). Yu (愚, simplicity) → zha (詐, deceit). Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: over time, the form of a virtue persists while its substance degrades.

refashioned: The Master said, 'Smooth words and a flattering appearance are rarely associated with virtue.'
elegant: Smooth words and a pleasing face: virtue's usual disguise — but usually a disguise for its absence.
distilled: The more polished the presentation, the more suspect the character.
notes: Qiao yan ling se, xian yi ren (巧言令色，鮮矣仁). Repeated from 1.3 — one of only a few sayings duplicated in the Analects. The repetition underscores how central this warning is to Confucius's teaching. Connects to FUALL's Reason: surface performance is inversely correlated with depth of character.

refashioned: The Master said, 'I hate the way purple displaces vermilion. I hate the way the songs of Zheng corrupt proper music. I hate those who use sharp tongues to overthrow states and families.'
elegant: Purple usurps red. Vulgar songs displace proper music. Sharp tongues topple kingdoms. Confucius hated all three — the corruption of the authentic by the counterfeit.
distilled: The counterfeit that displaces the genuine — in color, music, or speech — is the deepest form of corruption.
notes: Zi (紫, purple) displacing Zhu (朱, vermilion/red) — in Zhou court protocol, vermilion was the proper color of ritual garments; purple was an upstart imitation. Zheng sheng (鄭聲, songs of Zheng) — morally corrupting music. Li kou (利口, sharp tongues) — demagogues. All three examples are about counterfeits replacing originals. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: institutional decay happens when imitations successfully displace the genuine article.

refashioned: The Master said, 'I would prefer not to speak at all.' Zigong said, 'If you do not speak, Master, what will your disciples have to transmit?' The Master said, 'Does Heaven speak? The four seasons run their course, all things are born and grow, but does Heaven say anything?'
elegant: 'I would prefer silence.' 'Then what will we transmit?' 'Does Heaven speak? The seasons turn, life grows — and Heaven says nothing.'
distilled: The deepest truths may not require words; nature teaches without speaking.
notes: Tian he yan zai (天何言哉) — 'what does Heaven say?' This is Confucius's most Daoist-sounding statement, echoing Laozi's concept of wu wei and the Dao that cannot be spoken. But in context, Confucius is not abandoning language — he is noting that the deepest teachings may be transmitted through example and practice rather than verbal instruction. Connects to FUALL's framework: some knowledge is transmitted through practice (Li) rather than proposition.

refashioned: Ru Bei wanted to see Confucius. Confucius declined, claiming illness. But as soon as the messenger left the door, the Master picked up his lute and sang — making sure Ru Bei could hear.
elegant: Confucius told the visitor he was too sick to receive him — then picked up his lute and sang loudly enough for the visitor to hear the lie.
distilled: Sometimes the honest thing is to make your refusal unmistakable rather than politely ambiguous.
notes: Ru Bei (孺悲) — the visitor's identity is uncertain; some commentators suggest he was a person Confucius wished to rebuke. By singing audibly after claiming illness, Confucius deliberately reveals his own excuse as false — an act of transparent discourtesy that communicates his disapproval more powerfully than any explanation. This is Li (禮) used paradoxically: violating protocol to make a moral point.

refashioned:
Zai Wo argued that one year of mourning for parents was sufficient. 'If a gentleman abstains from propriety for three years, propriety will be lost. If he abstains from music for three years, music will be ruined. After a year, the old grain is consumed and new grain has grown. The seasonal cycle is complete. One year is enough.'

The Master asked, 'If you ate good rice and wore fine clothes after just one year, would you feel comfortable?' Zai Wo said, 'Yes.' The Master said, 'If you feel comfortable, then do it. But the superior person during mourning finds no pleasure in good food, no enjoyment in music, and no comfort in fine lodgings. That is why he does not do what you propose. Since you feel comfortable, go ahead.'

After Zai Wo left, the Master said, 'What a lack of virtue in Yu! A child does not leave its parents' arms until it is three years old. The three-year mourning period is observed throughout the world. Did Yu not receive three years of love from his parents?'
elegant: Zai Wo wanted to mourn for one year. Confucius asked: 'Would you feel comfortable eating well after one year?' 'Yes.' 'Then do it — but know that a virtuous person would not.' After he left: 'Did he not receive three years of love from his parents?'
distilled: The mourning period mirrors the years of helpless dependence we owed our parents — and cutting it short reveals the depth of our ingratitude.
notes: San nian zhi sang (三年之喪, three years' mourning) — one of the most debated Confucian practices. Zai Wo (宰我/宰予) — a disciple known for eloquence and for challenging Confucius. His rationalist argument (propriety decays, the grain cycle completes) is countered by Confucius's emotional argument: the three years mirror infancy's dependence. Xiao (孝, filial piety) — the mourning period is the child's reciprocal debt to parents. Confucius's method here is Socratic: he lets Zai Wo condemn himself. Connects to FUALL's framework: some obligations are grounded not in utility but in reciprocity and gratitude.

refashioned: The Master said, 'How hard it is to deal with the person who eats all day without applying his mind to anything worthwhile. Are there not board games and chess? Even those would be better than nothing.'
elegant: Even playing chess is better than spending the day eating and thinking about nothing.
distilled: Any mental exercise, even a game, is better than a mind that refuses to engage with anything.
notes: Bo yi (博弈, board games and chess) — games requiring strategy. Confucius does not endorse gaming but uses it as a floor: anything that exercises the mind is better than complete mental idleness. The passage is about the minimum standard of human engagement. Connects to FUALL's Academy: the first step is simply getting people to think about something.

refashioned: Zilu asked, 'Does the superior person esteem courage?' The Master said, 'The superior person holds righteousness supreme. A person in authority who has courage without righteousness will be guilty of rebellion. A common person who has courage without righteousness will become a robber.'
elegant: Courage without righteousness is rebellion in a leader and robbery in a commoner. Righteousness comes first.
distilled: Courage without moral direction is dangerous regardless of social position.
notes: Yi (義, righteousness) over Yong (勇, courage). The hierarchy is absolute: courage is only a virtue when governed by righteousness. Without Yi, courage in a ruler produces tyranny and in a subject produces crime. Connects to FUALL's Right: moral orientation must precede and constrain every other quality.

refashioned: Zigong asked, 'Does the superior person also have things he hates?' The Master said, 'Yes. He hates those who publicize others' faults. He hates those in low positions who slander their superiors. He hates the bold who lack propriety. He hates the decisive but narrow-minded.' The Master then asked, 'Zigong, do you also have your hatreds?' Zigong replied, 'I hate those who spy out information and call it wisdom. I hate those who mistake rudeness for courage. I hate those who expose secrets and call it honesty.'
elegant: The Master's hatreds: gossips, insubordinate slanderers, reckless brutes, and stubborn fools. Zigong's: spies who claim insight, bullies who claim courage, and tattlers who claim integrity.
distilled: Even the virtuous have hatreds — what they hate reveals what they value.
notes: A rich exchange about legitimate moral aversion. Both Confucius's and Zigong's lists target the same pattern: people who disguise vice as virtue. Calling espionage 'wisdom,' rudeness 'courage,' or betrayal 'honesty' — each is a corruption of Zhengming (正名, correct naming). Connects to FUALL's Reason: moral clarity requires seeing through self-serving relabelings of vice as virtue.

refashioned: The Master said, 'Women and servants are the most difficult to manage. If you are familiar with them, they become presumptuous. If you keep your distance, they become resentful.'
elegant: Familiarity breeds presumption; distance breeds resentment. Some relationships defy easy calibration.
distilled: Some social dynamics resist every strategy: closeness and distance both produce problems.
notes: This is the most controversial passage in the Analects in modern scholarship. Many scholars read it as reflecting the patriarchal norms of 5th-century BCE China rather than a timeless moral teaching. Others interpret 'women' (nü zi, 女子) as referring specifically to palace women and concubines, and 'servants' (xiao ren, 小人) as household staff — making it a comment on household management, not a universal claim about gender. The passage describes a genuine management dilemma about calibrating formality and warmth.

refashioned: The Master said, 'If a person is still disliked at forty, that is how he will always remain.'
elegant: If at forty you are still disliked, you are not going to change.
distilled: By forty, character is mostly fixed; the person who is still objectionable will likely stay that way.
notes: Si shi er jian wu yan (四十而見惡焉) — by age forty, character is set. This is descriptive rather than prescriptive: Confucius is not condemning change but observing its declining probability. Connects to 2.4 where Confucius describes his own development in decades (thirty: established; forty: no doubts; etc.).

refashioned: The Viscount of Wei withdrew from court. The Viscount of Ji became a slave. Bi Gan remonstrated with the tyrant and was killed. Confucius said, 'The Yin dynasty had these three men of virtue.'
elegant: One withdrew. One submitted to slavery. One spoke out and died. Confucius said: 'Three men of virtue.'
distilled: Virtue under tyranny takes many forms: withdrawal, endurance, and martyrdom are all legitimate responses.
notes: The Viscount of Wei (微子), the Viscount of Ji (箕子), and Bi Gan (比干) — three noble relatives of King Zhou (紂), the last and most tyrannical ruler of the Shang/Yin dynasty. Each responded to tyranny differently: Wei withdrew, Ji submitted to slavery, Bi Gan protested and was executed. Confucius validates all three responses. Connects to FUALL's framework: there is no single correct response to institutional corruption — integrity can express itself through flight, endurance, or confrontation.

refashioned: Hui of Liuxia served as chief judge and was dismissed three times. Someone said, 'Is it not time to leave?' He replied, 'If I serve people uprightly, where could I go and not be dismissed? If I were willing to serve dishonestly, why would I need to leave my own country?'
elegant: 'Serve honestly and be dismissed everywhere, or serve dishonestly and never need to leave.' He chose to stay and be dismissed.
distilled: The honest person who keeps getting fired is not the one with the problem.
notes: Hui of Liuxia (柳下惠) — a famous judge of Lu, repeatedly dismissed for refusing to compromise his principles. His logic is airtight: if integrity causes dismissal everywhere, the problem is systemic, not personal. If you abandon integrity, you can stay anywhere — but the staying is worthless. Connects to FUALL's framework: institutional corruption punishes integrity, but abandoning integrity to survive within the institution is worse than dismissal.

refashioned: Duke Jing of Qi, considering how to treat Confucius, said, 'I cannot give him the treatment due the head of the Ji family. I will treat him somewhere between that and the level of the Meng family.' He also said, 'I am old; I cannot put his doctrines to use.' Confucius departed.
elegant: The duke admitted he could not give Confucius top rank and was too old to use his ideas. So Confucius left.
distilled: When a ruler admits he cannot implement your teaching, there is nothing left to do but leave.
notes: Duke Jing of Qi (齊景公) — see 12.11 and 16.12. His half-hearted offer and admission of inability represent the frustration Confucius faced repeatedly: rulers who admired his ideas but lacked the will or capacity to implement them. Connects to FUALL's framework: ideas without institutional commitment remain ideas.

refashioned: The people of Qi sent a gift of female musicians to Lu. Ji Huan accepted them, and for three days the court did not convene. Confucius left.
elegant: Qi sent dancing girls. The court shut down for three days. Confucius packed and left.
distilled: When pleasure captures governance, the person of principle has no reason to stay.
notes: Ji Huan (季桓子) — head of the Ji family, the de facto ruler of Lu. The female musicians (nü yue, 女樂) were a deliberate diplomatic strategy by Qi to distract Lu's government. Confucius's departure was precipitated by this final evidence that Lu's rulers were unserious. This occurred around 497 BCE, beginning Confucius's 14-year exile. Connects to FUALL's erosion pattern: when entertainment displaces governance, the system has failed.

refashioned: Jieyu, the madman of Chu, passed by Confucius singing: 'O Phoenix! O Phoenix! How your virtue has declined! The past cannot be corrected, but the future may still be saved. Give up! Give up! Those who serve in government today court danger.' Confucius got down from his carriage to speak with him, but Jieyu hurried away before they could talk.
elegant: A madman sang to Confucius: 'O Phoenix, your virtue is fading. Give up — government now is peril.' Confucius ran after him, but the madman was already gone.
distilled: Sometimes the clearest assessment of a situation comes from those considered mad — and the sage cannot even catch them to respond.
notes: Jieyu (接舆) — the 'madman of Chu,' a famous recluse figure who appears in both the Analects and the Zhuangzi. The phoenix (feng, 鳳) is Confucius's symbol — a mythical bird that appears only in times of good governance. Jieyu's song is both warning and lament: the age of good governance has passed. Confucius's attempt to engage and Jieyu's refusal dramatize the split between activism and withdrawal. Connects to FUALL's framework: the tension between the activist who insists on engagement and the critic who sees the futility.

refashioned:
Changju and Jieni were plowing together when Confucius passed by and sent Zilu to ask for directions to the river ford. Changju asked, 'Who is riding in that carriage?' 'Kong Qiu.' 'Of Lu?' 'Yes.'
